content.text: A shared message bus decouples publishers from subscribers behind a central routing layer. Instead of agents calling each other directly, each agent publishes typed events to named topics; any other agent that has subscribed to that topic receives a copy. This publish-subscribe model makes it straightforward to add new consumers — an audit logger, an alerting agent, a real-time dashboard — without touching existing publisher code.

attrs.title: Publishers push events to the bus; the bus fans out to every matching subscriber queue

content.text: When to Use a Shared Bus
Multiple consumers need the same event stream (e.g., both an alerting agent and an audit logger consume error events).
The set of subscribers is not known at authoring time — new agents must be able to join without modifying publishers.
You want temporal decoupling: a publisher should not block waiting for a slow consumer to finish processing.
System-wide observability is required — the bus acts as a single tap point for monitoring all inter-agent communication.
A direct Send-based pattern (one publisher, one consumer) is simpler when the fanout factor is always one. Upgrade to a bus when you genuinely have multiple consumers of the same event.
Events on the bus must carry enough metadata for subscribers to filter and correlate them without needing to call back into the publisher:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any, Literal
import uuid
from datetime import datetime, timezone


TOPIC = Literal["findings", "insights", "alerts", "status", "__all__"]


@dataclass
class EventMessage:
    event_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    topic: str = ""               # routing key, e.g. "findings"
    publisher: str = ""           # originating agent id
    payload: dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
    timestamp: str = field(
        default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    )
    correlation_id: str = ""      # links events across a single task run
In a LangGraph context the bus is a plain Python object shared by all agent closures — not a separate process. Subscribers register an
per topic; when the bus receives an event it enqueues a copy into every matching queue. Because LangGraph runs graph steps sequentially (unless using Send parallelism), an
provides clean hand-off even in synchronous code by using

import asyncio
from collections import defaultdict
from copy import deepcopy


class MessageBus:
    """In-process pub-sub bus for multi-agent graphs."""

    def __init__(self):
        # topic -> list of subscriber queues
        self._subscribers: dict[str, list[asyncio.Queue]] = defaultdict(list)

    def subscribe(self, topic: str) -> asyncio.Queue:
        """Register interest in a topic; returns the delivery queue."""
        q: asyncio.Queue = asyncio.Queue()
        self._subscribers[topic].append(q)
        # "__all__" topic receives every event regardless of topic
        if topic != "__all__":
            self._subscribers["__all__"]
        return q

    def subscribe_all(self) -> asyncio.Queue:
        """Receive every event published to any topic."""
        return self.subscribe("__all__")

    def publish(self, event: EventMessage) -> int:
        """Deliver event to all matching queues. Returns delivery count."""
        delivered = 0
        # specific topic subscribers
        for q in self._subscribers.get(event.topic, []):
            q.put_nowait(deepcopy(event))
            delivered += 1
        # wildcard subscribers
        for q in self._subscribers.get("__all__", []):
            q.put_nowait(deepcopy(event))
            delivered += 1
        return delivered

    def drain(self, q: asyncio.Queue) -> list[EventMessage]:
        """Non-blocking: collect all currently queued events."""
        events = []
        while not q.empty():
            events.append(q.get_nowait())
        return events
Each agent node closure captures its queue at construction time. Publishers call
at the start of their node function to consume waiting events:

import operator
from typing import Annotated
from typing_extensions import TypedDict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic


class BusState(TypedDict):
    topic: str                              # research question
    findings: Annotated[list[str], operator.add]
    insights: Annotated[list[str], operator.add]
    alerts: Annotated[list[str], operator.add]
    final_report: str


# Shared bus instance — lives outside the graph
bus = MessageBus()
findings_q = bus.subscribe("findings")
insights_q = bus.subscribe("insights")
alerts_q   = bus.subscribe("alerts")
audit_q    = bus.subscribe_all()


def research_agent(state: BusState) -> dict:
    llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")
    result = llm.invoke(f"Research: {state['topic']}")
    event = EventMessage(
        topic="findings",
        publisher="research_agent",
        payload={"text": result.content},
    )
    bus.publish(event)
    return {"findings": [result.content]}


def analysis_agent(state: BusState) -> dict:
    # consume findings published by research_agent
    events = bus.drain(findings_q)
    if not events:
        return {"insights": []}
    llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251011")
    combined = " ".join(e.payload["text"] for e in events)
    result = llm.invoke(f"Analyse: {combined}")
    bus.publish(EventMessage(
        topic="insights",
        publisher="analysis_agent",
        payload={"text": result.content},
    ))
    return {"insights": [result.content]}


def audit_logger(state: BusState) -> dict:
    """Passively log every event — no LLM call needed."""
    events = bus.drain(audit_q)
    for ev in events:
        print(f"[AUDIT] {ev.timestamp} topic={ev.topic} publisher={ev.publisher}")
    return {}  # audit logger never modifies state


def synthesis_agent(state: BusState) -> dict:
    events = bus.drain(insights_q)
    texts = [e.payload["text"] for e in events] + state.get("insights", [])
    if not texts:
        return {"final_report": "No insights to synthesise."}
    llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-5")
    report = llm.invoke("Write a concise report:\n" + "\n".join(texts))
    return {"final_report": report.content}


builder = StateGraph(BusState)
for name, fn in [
    ("research", research_agent),
    ("analysis", analysis_agent),
    ("audit", audit_logger),
    ("synthesis", synthesis_agent),
]:
    builder.add_node(name, fn)

builder.set_entry_point("research")
builder.add_edge("research", "analysis")
builder.add_edge("analysis", "audit")
builder.add_edge("audit", "synthesis")
builder.add_edge("synthesis", END)

graph = builder.compile()
In systems with many event types, topic strings alone may be too coarse. Add a predicate layer so subscribers receive only the events that satisfy a condition:
to apply backpressure and prevent memory exhaustion when a slow subscriber falls behind.
— the bus preserves per-queue FIFO order (asyncio.Queue is FIFO). Cross-topic ordering is not guaranteed; use the
field if consumers need to re-order across topics.
— the bus is a stateful object. Drain all queues or create a fresh bus instance between LangGraph invocations to avoid events leaking across runs.
— in-process queues are not serialisable to LangGraph's checkpoint store. If you need resumable runs, store events in the graph state (an
field) as the durable record; use the bus purely for in-flight delivery.

— for distributed agents running in separate processes, replace the in-process bus with Redis Streams, Kafka, or Google Pub/Sub. The EventMessage schema and topic conventions remain the same; only the transport changes.

Testing agents that communicate through a bus is cleaner than testing direct-call graphs because each agent can be tested in isolation by injecting pre-built events into its input queue, without running the other agents at all.

import asyncio
import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock


def test_analysis_agent_reads_findings():
    """analysis_agent should consume findings and publish insights."""
    test_bus = FilteredMessageBus()
    findings_q = test_bus.subscribe("findings")
    insights_q = test_bus.subscribe("insights")

    # Pre-load a finding
    test_bus.publish(EventMessage(
        topic="findings",
        publisher="test",
        payload={"text": "Python is a popular language for AI."},
    ))

    # Mock the LLM so the test doesn't make a real API call
    mock_llm = MagicMock()
    mock_llm.invoke.return_value = MagicMock(
        content="AI adoption is rising among Python developers."
    )

    with patch("ChatAnthropic", return_value=mock_llm):
        state = analysis_agent({"topic": "AI trends"})

    assert len(state["insights"]) == 1
    assert "AI adoption" in state["insights"][0]

    # Also verify the event was published back onto the bus
    published = test_bus.drain(insights_q)
    assert len(published) == 1
    assert published[0].publisher == "analysis_agent"


def test_audit_logger_captures_all_events():
    """Audit logger should see every event on any topic."""
    test_bus = FilteredMessageBus()
    all_q = test_bus.subscribe_all()

    test_bus.publish(EventMessage(topic="findings", publisher="a", payload={}))
    test_bus.publish(EventMessage(topic="alerts", publisher="b", payload={}))

    captured = test_bus.drain(all_q)
    assert len(captured) == 2
    topics = {e.topic for e in captured}
    assert topics == {"findings", "alerts"}
described above is a thin shim. When agents must run in separate processes or containers, swap the internal queues for an external broker while keeping the same
to JSON for the wire format. The consuming agent deserialises it and continues processing identically to the in-process variant — only the
A shared message bus decouples publishers from subscribers by routing typed EventMessages through named topics.
with the bus; the bus fans out a deep copy of every matching event.
for cross-cutting concerns like audit logging and monitoring without touching any publisher.
Add predicate filters when topic granularity is insufficient — subscribers only receive events their predicate accepts.
state fields for checkpointing; use the in-process bus only for delivery.
Prefer direct Send for one-to-one tasks; upgrade to a bus when fan-out exceeds one consumer or consumers are added at runtime.
